topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the blueprint spread across the worn oak table, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead seeming to amplify the silence in the lab. He traced the exact contour of the schematic with a fingertip, his breath catching in his throat. Beside him, Clara was meticulously aligning a component, her movements precise, almost unnervingly so. He glanced up, his eyes flicking from the paper to her face, a sudden, cold knot tightening in his chest. It was the same iteration. Every wire diagram, every stress calculation, every theoretical breakthrough—it was his, and it was hers. A tremor ran through his hand as he pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal on concrete sounding deafeningly loud. He needed air. He needed distance.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," Marcus muttered, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. He leaned back from the console, the cool plastic of the equipment offering no comfort against the sudden heat rising in his neck. Across the room, Lena was adjusting the calibration settings on the identical sensor array. They were running parallel processes, executing the same sequence of commands, generating the same preliminary data. Marcus felt a distinct pressure behind his eyes, a dizzying sense of disorientation. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small perimeter of the workspace, his footsteps echoing hollowly. He kept catching glimpses of Lena’s focused profile, and a strange, hollow echo resonated in his skull.

[story 3]

"Are you sure about this configuration?" David asked, his voice tight, barely above a whisper. He looked down at the physical prototype sitting on the workbench—a sleek, unfamiliar device. Then he turned to Sarah, who was examining the diagnostic report on her screen. The data streams matched perfectly. The algorithms, the material specifications, even the slight imperfections in the initial fabrication—it was a mirror image of his own work, only executed by another set of hands. David gripped the edge of the bench, his knuckles turning white. He took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the frantic drumming in his chest. He couldn't look away from the reflection of his own ambition staring back at him in Sarah's steady gaze.
